Good morning, everyone. Here is a short update on our biggest project of the year. Our first milestone, the signed contract, is now complete, and every stakeholder confirmed in writing. The scope is clear: a supply agreement for two countries. My own team is accountable for the next deliverable, and we have already started it. We have reached two milestones so far, and we are on schedule. I want to be honest about the timeline, so I promise good follow-through. We will meet every commitment on time. This deal has been hard work, but it shows what we can do together. Thank you all for your effort, and let us keep this energy until the final signature.

Let me report how we closed our last big deal. At the first meeting, the client said the scope was too wide. Our director told them that we could split the work into clear milestones. The client said they needed a realistic timeline, and we told them we would meet every milestone on time. Then we made a simple offer: if they accepted the scope, we would start the project the next week. They said the plan was fair, and they confirmed. Every stakeholder signed, and each side kept a copy. The client told me later that our clear commitment and good follow-through closed the deal. So remember this: words win deals, but follow-through keeps them.

First, the team presents the scope and the first milestone to every stakeholder. Then, they agree on a realistic timeline for the project. Next, one director makes a conditional offer: if you accept, we will start next week. After that, both sides confirm the commitment and sign the agreement. Finally, the team reports the deal and keeps good follow-through on every deliverable.
